questions.stem: A nurse is admitting a 79-year-old patient with a history of orthostatic hypotension, recent fall, and current use of furosemide and lisinopril. Which intervention is HIGHEST priority for fall prevention?

options.text: Place a fall-risk bracelet on the patient and document in the chart
Educate the patient to call for help before getting out of bed and place the call light within reach
Request a physical therapy consult for gait evaluation
Move the patient to a room closer to the nursing station

questions.rationale: The highest-impact, immediately actionable fall prevention measure is patient education combined with environmental setup: instructing the patient to call for assistance before ambulating and ensuring the call light is accessible. This directly addresses the behavioral mechanism of most falls (attempting to get up unassisted) and is executable immediately at the bedside. Applying a fall-risk bracelet without the behavioral intervention does not prevent falls. PT consult and room relocation are appropriate secondary measures but do not provide immediate direct protection.

distractorRationales.a: Place a fall-risk bracelet on the patient and document in the chart misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control Fall Prevention infection points to Educate the patient to call for help before getting out of bed and place the call, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Request a physical therapy consult for gait evaluation misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control Fall Prevention infection points to Educate the patient to call for help before getting out of bed and place the call, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Move the patient to a room closer to the nursing station misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control Fall Prevention infection points to Educate the patient to call for help before getting out of bed and place the call, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Fall prevention priority: immediate behavioral intervention (call before rising, call light in reach) > documentation/labeling alone. Identification of risk means nothing without active mitigation at the point of care.

visualRationale.caption: Fall prevention priority: immediate behavioral intervention (call before rising, call light in reach) > documentation/labeling alone. Identification of risk means nothing without active mitigation at the point of care.
visualRationale.conclusion: Fall prevention priority: immediate behavioral intervention (call before rising, call light in reach) > documentation/labeling alone. Identification of risk means nothing without active mitigation at the point of care.

questions.stem: A nurse is about to administer a scheduled dose of digoxin 0.25 mg IV to a patient with heart failure. The patient's apical heart rate is 54 bpm. What should the nurse do FIRST?

options.text: Administer the dose as scheduled since the order is valid
Hold the dose and notify the provider of the patient's heart rate
Administer half the dose and recheck the heart rate in 30 minutes
Check the patient's most recent serum digoxin level before acting

questions.rationale: The standard safety parameter for digoxin is to hold the dose and notify the provider if the apical pulse is < 60 bpm in adults. Digoxin slows AV conduction; administering it to a patient with a heart rate of 54 bpm risks further bradycardia, heart block, and cardiovascular compromise. Administering the full dose ignores a critical safety parameter. Administering half the dose is not within standard nursing scope without a specific provider order. While checking the digoxin level is part of toxicity monitoring, the immediate safety action is to hold and notify.

distractorRationales.a: Administer the dose as scheduled since the order is valid misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control Medication Safety infection points to Hold the dose and notify the provider of the patient's heart rate, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Administer half the dose and recheck the heart rate in 30 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control Medication Safety infection points to Hold the dose and notify the provider of the patient's heart rate, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Check the patient's most recent serum digoxin level before acting misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control Medication Safety infection points to Hold the dose and notify the provider of the patient's heart rate, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Digoxin safety rule: Hold and notify if apical HR < 60 bpm (adults). This applies regardless of whether the serum level is available. Safety parameters come before scheduled administration.

questions.stem: During delivery, after the baby's head delivers, the anterior shoulder fails to deliver. The turtle sign is present (head retracts against the perineum). Which intervention is attempted FIRST?

options.text: Apply fundal pressure to push the baby down
McRoberts maneuver: hyperflexion of maternal thighs against the abdomen with suprapubic pressure
Zavanelli maneuver: cephalic replacement followed by emergency cesarean
Perform an episiotomy to enlarge the vaginal opening

questions.rationale: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency. The McRoberts maneuver (sharply flexing maternal thighs onto the abdomen) flattens the lumbar lordosis and rotates the pubic symphysis superiorly, increasing the functional AP diameter of the pelvis. Combined with suprapubic pressure (pushing the anterior shoulder downward and laterally), this resolves approximately 90% of cases. Fundal pressure (A) is absolutely contraindicated — it worsens impaction of the shoulder. Zavanelli maneuver (C) is a last resort when all other maneuvers fail. Episiotomy (D) alone does not help shoulder dystocia as the obstruction is bony, not soft tissue.

distractorRationales.a: Apply fundal pressure to push the baby down misses the clinical pattern: Shoulder Dystocia safety points to McRoberts maneuver: hyperflexion of maternal thighs against the abdomen with suprapubic pressure, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Zavanelli maneuver: cephalic replacement followed by emergency cesarean misses the clinical pattern: Shoulder Dystocia safety points to McRoberts maneuver: hyperflexion of maternal thighs against the abdomen with suprapubic pressure, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Perform an episiotomy to enlarge the vaginal opening misses the clinical pattern: Shoulder Dystocia safety points to McRoberts maneuver: hyperflexion of maternal thighs against the abdomen with suprapubic pressure, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Shoulder dystocia: McRoberts + suprapubic pressure first; fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated — it worsens the impaction.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a client who says: 'I've been thinking about ending my life. I have my grandfather's gun at home and I plan to use it this weekend when my family is away.' Which suicide risk category applies, and what is the immediate nursing priority?

options.text: Low risk; schedule outpatient follow-up within 2 weeks
Moderate risk; increase therapy frequency and provide a safety plan
High risk; initiate involuntary hospitalization and remove access to means immediately
Moderate risk; contact family members to monitor the patient at home

questions.rationale: This patient has a specific plan (gun), access to lethal means (gun at home), a timeframe (this weekend), and intent (plans to use it). This constellation — ideation + plan + means + timeframe — represents HIGH lethality risk. Immediate action: do not leave the patient alone, initiate emergency psychiatric evaluation, and facilitate involuntary hospitalization if the patient refuses voluntary admission. Means restriction (removing or securing the firearm) is critical. Outpatient follow-up (A) and home monitoring (D) are wholly inadequate for this risk level.

distractorRationales.a: Low risk; schedule outpatient follow-up within 2 weeks misses the clinical pattern: Suicide Risk Assessment assessment points to High risk; initiate involuntary hospitalization and remove access to means immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.b: Moderate risk; increase therapy frequency and provide a safety plan misses the clinical pattern: Suicide Risk Assessment assessment points to High risk; initiate involuntary hospitalization and remove access to means immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: Moderate risk; contact family members to monitor the patient at home misses the clinical pattern: Suicide Risk Assessment assessment points to High risk; initiate involuntary hospitalization and remove access to means immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: High suicide risk = specific plan + lethal means + timeframe + intent; immediate hospitalization and means restriction are required.

questions.stem: The charge nurse on a medical unit receives three simultaneous calls. Which situation should be addressed FIRST?

options.text: A nurse reports that a patient's blood glucose is 52 mg/dL and the patient is awake, oriented, and able to swallow
A nurse reports that a patient post-CVA is refusing to eat lunch and is becoming tearful
A nurse reports that a patient on IV heparin has a new complaint of severe headache rated 10/10 and sudden right-sided weakness
A family member at the desk is upset because their loved one has not had a bath yet today

questions.rationale: A patient on heparin who develops sudden severe headache (10/10, 'worst of my life' quality) and new focal neurological deficit (right-sided weakness) has clinical signs of intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) until proven otherwise. This is a medical emergency: heparin must be stopped immediately, a STAT CT head ordered, and reversal (protamine sulfate) considered. Heparin-induced ICH carries high mortality if not acted upon within minutes. Hypoglycemia (A) requires treatment but the patient is conscious and can take oral glucose — not immediately life-threatening. The CVA patient (B) needs emotional support but is stable. The family complaint (D) is non-urgent.

distractorRationales.a: A nurse reports that a patient's blood glucose is 52 mg/dL and the patient is awake, misses the clinical pattern: Safety Priority Which Patient First hemorrhage points to A nurse reports that a patient on IV heparin has a new complaint of severe headache, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.b: A nurse reports that a patient post-CVA is refusing to eat lunch and is becoming tearful misses the clinical pattern: Safety Priority Which Patient First hemorrhage points to A nurse reports that a patient on IV heparin has a new complaint of severe headache, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: A family member at the desk is upset because their loved one has not had a misses the clinical pattern: Safety Priority Which Patient First hemorrhage points to A nurse reports that a patient on IV heparin has a new complaint of severe headache, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Anticoagulated patient + sudden severe headache + new focal neuro deficit = suspected ICH. Stop anticoagulation, get CT STAT. This is a higher priority than hypoglycemia in an alert patient who can self-treat orally.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue misses the clinical pattern: Acute Angle Glaucoma pain points to The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home misses the clinical pattern: Acute Angle Glaucoma pain points to The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well misses the clinical pattern: Acute Angle Glaucoma pain points to The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight misses the clinical pattern: Adrenal Crisis Hypotension sodium points to Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn misses the clinical pattern: Adrenal Crisis Hypotension sodium points to Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Teach the client about low-sodium foods misses the clinical pattern: Adrenal Crisis Hypotension sodium points to Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: Obtain a full allergy history before intervening misses the clinical pattern: Anaphylaxis Epinephrine First airway points to Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction misses the clinical pattern: Anaphylaxis Epinephrine First airway points to Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Document the event and continue monitoring misses the clinical pattern: Anaphylaxis Epinephrine First airway points to Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature misses the clinical pattern: Autonomic Dysreflexia Bladder hypertension points to Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes misses the clinical pattern: Autonomic Dysreflexia Bladder hypertension points to Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Autonomic Dysreflexia Bladder hypertension points to Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes misses the clinical pattern: Blood Transfusion Reaction transfusion points to Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion misses the clinical pattern: Blood Transfusion Reaction transfusion points to Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response misses the clinical pattern: Blood Transfusion Reaction transfusion points to Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster misses the clinical pattern: C Diff Spore Precautions infection points to Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Place the client on droplet precautions only misses the clinical pattern: C Diff Spore Precautions infection points to Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel misses the clinical pattern: C Diff Spore Precautions infection points to Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort misses the clinical pattern: Central Line Infection Prevention infection points to Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures misses the clinical pattern: Central Line Infection Prevention infection points to Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions misses the clinical pattern: Central Line Infection Prevention infection points to Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.
